measurements.measurement_reference: Front door ~2.1m, ~28 courses. These are single-storey cottages so total wall height from grade to eave is approximately 2.7-3.0m (~36-40 courses).

brick_analysis.bond_notes: Standard half-lap running bond throughout all units. Consistent coursing across the row.

colour.description: Medium red-orange brick. Some units show slightly different tones — the rightmost unit (136) appears slightly more brown/weathered while the inner units are warmer orange-red. This may reflect different repair/replacement brick or simply different sun exposure. Overall consistent with standard Kensington residential brick.

special_features.description: Each cottage has its own peaked front-facing gable, creating a repeating sawtooth roofline. The gables are small and steep, typical of Gothic cottage form. Some gables have decorative vergeboard trim.
Simple flat brick lintels above all openings.

condition.spalling_locations: Some spalling on individual bricks, particularly near grade level
condition.notes: Row of four single-storey worker's cottages with peaked gables — a distinctive and increasingly rare building type in the HCD. These represent the most modest residential construction in the neighbourhood, likely dating from the 1880s-1890s. The small scale means the total brick wall area per unit is relatively small (approximately 2.7-3.0m grade to eave). Large tree partially obscures the two centre units. Parked cars and chain-link fences in front. The building type is important for the 3D model as it represents the lowest-scale residential form in the district.

texture_calibration.notes: Door reference used for coursing. The single-storey scale means fewer courses are visible (only 36-40 courses total from grade to eave). Tree and cars obstruct portions. Brick measurements consistent with neighbourhood baseline — same brick supply as nearby 2-storey houses.
